name: Ashwagandha
title: Withania somnifera — WHO Monograph
text: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is an adaptogenic herb used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 3000 years. The root is the primary medicinal part, containing withanolides as the principal bioactive constituents. Clinical studies have demonstrated that ashwagandha root extract significantly reduces serum cortisol levels and improves stress resilience in adults with chronic stress. A randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial showed a 30 percent reduction in cortisol levels after 60 days of supplementation at 300 mg twice daily. The herb has also shown anxiolytic effects comparable to lorazepam in animal models without the sedative side effects. Ashwagandha exhibits immunomodulatory properties by enhancing natural killer cell activity and increasing immunoglobulin production. It has demonstrated neuroprotective effects in preclinical studies, promoting neuronal regeneration and reducing beta-amyloid plaque formation. Traditional uses include treatment of general debility, emaciation, impotence, and premature aging. The recommended dosage is 300-600 mg of standardized root extract daily. Contraindications include pregnancy due to potential abortifacient effects. Interactions with thyroid medications, immunosuppressants, and sedatives have been reported. Common side effects include mild gastrointestinal discomfort at higher doses. The safety profile is generally favorable for short-term use up to 3 months. In a 2019 clinical trial involving 60 participants, ashwagandha supplementation at 600 mg daily for 8 weeks resulted in significant improvements in sleep quality as measured by the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index. Participants reported reduced sleep onset latency and increased total sleep time. The study also observed improvements in mental alertness upon waking. Ashwagandha has been investigated for its effects on male reproductive health, with studies showing increased testosterone levels and improved semen quality in infertile men. A 2013 study found that 5 grams of root powder daily for 3 months increased sperm count and motility. The herb has demonstrated cardioprotective effects through reduction of oxidative stress markers and improvement of lipid profiles in preclinical models. Research into ashwagandha for exercise performance has shown enhanced maximal oxygen consumption and endurance in trained athletes. The anti-cancer potential has been explored in cell culture and animal studies, with withanolides demonstrating pro-apoptotic and anti-angiogenic properties. Quality control is important as commercially available products vary significantly in withanolide content. The KSM-66 and Sensoril extracts are the most clinically studied standardized preparations.

name: Turmeric
title: Curcuma longa — WHO Monograph
text: Turmeric (Curcuma longa) rhizome has been used in traditional medicine systems across South and Southeast Asia for centuries. The primary bioactive compound is curcumin, a polyphenol responsible for the characteristic yellow color and most pharmacological activities. Curcumin has demonstrated potent anti-inflammatory activity by inhibiting NF-kB, COX-2, and lipoxygenase pathways. Clinical trials have shown efficacy in osteoarthritis, with improvements in pain scores comparable to ibuprofen. The compound exhibits antioxidant properties through direct free radical scavenging and upregulation of endogenous antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase and catalase. Curcumin shows promise in metabolic syndrome by improving insulin sensitivity and reducing fasting blood glucose. Bioavailability is a significant limitation, as curcumin undergoes rapid hepatic metabolism and intestinal elimination. Combining with piperine from black pepper increases bioavailability by 2000 percent through inhibition of glucuronidation. The recommended dosage is 500-2000 mg of standardized turmeric extract containing 95 percent curcuminoids daily. Contraindications include gallbladder disease and concurrent anticoagulant therapy. Turmeric may potentiate the effects of blood-thinning medications. Safety data supports use up to 8 grams daily for short periods. Curcumin has been extensively studied for its neuroprotective properties, with research suggesting it may cross the blood-brain barrier and reduce neuroinflammation associated with Alzheimer disease. Epidemiological studies in India, where turmeric consumption is high, show lower rates of neurodegenerative disease. The compound has demonstrated antidepressant effects in clinical trials, with some studies showing efficacy comparable to fluoxetine for major depressive disorder. In oncology research, curcumin has shown ability to modulate multiple molecular targets involved in cancer development, including growth factors, transcription factors, and apoptotic proteins. Phase I and II clinical trials have explored curcumin in colorectal cancer, pancreatic cancer, and multiple myeloma, with encouraging safety profiles. Curcumin also shows hepatoprotective effects and has been studied for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease with positive preliminary results. The compound has demonstrated antimicrobial activity against various bacteria and fungi. Recent research has explored nano-formulations and liposomal delivery systems to overcome the bioavailability challenge. Phytosome technology has shown particular promise in clinical settings.

name: Ginger
title: Zingiber officinale — WHO Monograph
text: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) rhizome is among the most widely used medicinal plants globally. The principal bioactive compounds are gingerols and shogaols, which are responsible for the pungent taste and therapeutic effects. Ginger has demonstrated strong antiemetic properties in multiple clinical settings. Randomized controlled trials confirm efficacy for pregnancy-related nausea and vomiting at doses of 1 gram daily, with no adverse effects on pregnancy outcomes. It is also effective for chemotherapy-induced nausea when used as adjunct therapy. The anti-inflammatory mechanism involves inhibition of prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis. Clinical studies show modest pain reduction in osteoarthritis patients using 500 mg to 1 gram daily. Ginger stimulates gastric motility and has carminative properties, making it useful for functional dyspepsia. The spice demonstrates antiplatelet activity in vitro, though clinical significance at dietary doses is uncertain. Recommended therapeutic dosage is 500 mg to 2 grams of dried ginger powder daily. Contraindications include gallstone disease. Caution is advised with concurrent anticoagulant use at high supplemental doses. The WHO considers ginger generally safe for use during pregnancy at recommended doses. Research has also explored ginger's role in glucose metabolism, with several clinical trials demonstrating reductions in fasting blood glucose and HbA1c in type 2 diabetes patients. A meta-analysis of 10 randomized controlled trials found significant improvements in glycemic control. Ginger has shown cardioprotective potential through reduction of cholesterol levels and inhibition of platelet aggregation. The analgesic properties have been studied for primary dysmenorrhea, with ginger showing efficacy comparable to mefenamic acid and ibuprofen. Antimicrobial studies demonstrate activity against foodborne pathogens including Salmonella and Listeria. The essential oil has shown antifungal activity against Candida species. Traditional use in Ayurvedic and Traditional Chinese Medicine includes treatment of respiratory infections, where ginger is believed to promote expectoration and reduce congestion. Modern pharmacological research supports the bronchodilatory and mucolytic properties. Fresh and dried ginger contain different proportions of gingerols and shogaols, with dried ginger having higher shogaol content due to dehydration of gingerols.

name: Echinacea
title: Echinacea purpurea — WHO Monograph
text: Echinacea (Echinacea purpurea, E. angustifolia, E. pallida) encompasses several species used in herbal medicine for immune support. The active constituents include alkamides, caffeic acid derivatives such as cichoric acid, and polysaccharides. Echinacea preparations have been extensively studied for prevention and treatment of upper respiratory tract infections. Meta-analyses suggest a modest reduction in cold duration by approximately 1.4 days and a 10-20 percent reduction in cold incidence with prophylactic use. The immunomodulatory mechanism involves stimulation of phagocytosis, increased production of cytokines including TNF-alpha and interleukins, and enhanced natural killer cell activity. Different species and plant parts show varying potencies, with aerial parts of E. purpurea being most commonly studied. The recommended dosage varies by preparation: standardized extract 300-500 mg three times daily at symptom onset. Duration of continuous use should not exceed 8 weeks. Contraindications include autoimmune diseases, progressive systemic diseases such as tuberculosis, and known allergy to Asteraceae family plants. Adverse effects are uncommon but may include allergic reactions and mild gastrointestinal symptoms. Beyond respiratory infections, echinacea has been studied for wound healing, with topical preparations showing enhanced collagen synthesis and tissue regeneration in animal models. The anti-inflammatory effects may benefit conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, though clinical evidence is limited. Some research suggests anxiolytic properties, with specific alkamide fractions showing activity at cannabinoid CB2 receptors. Echinacea has demonstrated antiviral activity in vitro against influenza viruses, herpes simplex, and respiratory syncytial virus. The herb has been investigated for potential benefits in chemotherapy-induced leukopenia, with some positive preliminary clinical results. Quality control is critical as market products vary widely in species composition, plant part used, and active compound concentrations. Adulteration with Parthenium integrifolium has been documented.

name: Ginkgo
title: Ginkgo biloba — WHO Monograph
text: Ginkgo biloba leaf extract is one of the most widely studied herbal medicines globally, with extensive clinical trial data. The standardized extract EGb 761 contains 24 percent flavonoid glycosides and 6 percent terpene lactones including ginkgolides and bilobalide. Ginkgo improves cerebral blood flow and demonstrates neuroprotective properties through antioxidant activity and PAF antagonism. Clinical trials in dementia patients show modest improvements in cognitive function and daily living activities at doses of 120-240 mg daily. The extract has shown efficacy in peripheral arterial disease, increasing pain-free walking distance in intermittent claudication. Ginkgo demonstrates antiplatelet effects through PAF receptor antagonism, which contributes to both therapeutic benefits and bleeding risk. Visual function improvements have been noted in normal tension glaucoma and age-related macular degeneration studies. The recommended dosage is 120-240 mg of standardized extract daily, divided into two or three doses. Onset of effect typically requires 4-6 weeks of consistent use. Contraindications include bleeding disorders and concurrent anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy. Reported side effects include headache, gastrointestinal disturbance, and rare cases of bleeding. The GEM (Ginkgo Evaluation of Memory) trial, a large randomized controlled study involving over 3000 elderly participants, found no benefit for preventing dementia or cognitive decline in healthy older adults. However, subgroup analyses suggested possible benefits in participants with existing mild cognitive impairment. Ginkgo has demonstrated cardioprotective effects through improvement of endothelial function and reduction of oxidative stress. In tinnitus and vertigo, clinical evidence is mixed but some studies show symptomatic improvement with prolonged use. The herb has been studied for sexual dysfunction, particularly SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction, with some positive pilot studies. Safety concerns include potential interactions with anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, and seizure threshold-lowering medications. Ginkgo seed ingestion has been associated with seizures and should be distinguished from leaf extract use.

name: Garlic
title: Allium sativum — WHO Monograph
text: Garlic (Allium sativum) bulb has been used medicinally for over 5000 years across numerous cultures. The principal bioactive compound allicin is produced when alliin is converted by the enzyme alliinase upon crushing or cutting. Garlic has demonstrated consistent cardiovascular benefits in clinical trials. Meta-analyses show a modest reduction in systolic blood pressure of 6-8 mmHg with daily supplementation equivalent to 3.6-5.4 mg allicin. The lipid-lowering effect includes approximately 10-15 percent reduction in total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol. Garlic exhibits broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against bacteria, fungi, viruses and parasites. The antiatherosclerotic mechanism involves inhibition of cholesterol synthesis, reduction of LDL oxidation, and improvement of endothelial function. Antithrombotic effects include inhibition of platelet aggregation through multiple pathways. Some epidemiological evidence suggests reduced risk of gastric and colorectal cancers with regular garlic consumption. The recommended dosage is 2-5 grams of fresh garlic or 300-1000 mg of standardized garlic powder daily. Contraindications include pre-surgical states due to increased bleeding risk. Drug interactions include potentiation of anticoagulants and interference with HIV protease inhibitors. Aged garlic extract has been the subject of numerous clinical trials, demonstrating cardiovascular benefits distinct from fresh garlic preparations. The aging process converts allicin into more stable organosulfur compounds including S-allylcysteine, which has demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Clinical trials have shown aged garlic extract may reduce coronary artery calcium progression and improve endothelial function. Garlic's immune-enhancing properties have been demonstrated in clinical studies showing reduced frequency and duration of common cold episodes. The antimicrobial activity extends to antibiotic-resistant organisms including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Preclinical research has explored garlic compounds for hepatoprotective effects and as adjuncts to cancer chemotherapy. The variability in commercial garlic supplement quality remains a concern, with studies showing significant differences in allicin yield between products.

name: Valerian
title: Valeriana officinalis — WHO Monograph
text: Valerian (Valeriana officinalis) root has been used as a sedative and anxiolytic since ancient Greek and Roman times. The active constituents include valerenic acid, isovaleric acid, and iridoids known as valepotriates. Valerian modulates GABA-A receptor activity, increasing GABAergic transmission in the central nervous system. Clinical trials for insomnia show improvements in subjective sleep quality, particularly sleep latency, with regular use over 2-4 weeks. Unlike benzodiazepines, valerian does not produce morning drowsiness or dependence at recommended doses. The anxiolytic effect has been demonstrated in clinical studies, though evidence is less robust than for sleep applications. Valerian may enhance the effects of other sedative herbs when used in combination formulas with hops or passionflower. The recommended dosage for sleep is 300-600 mg of standardized extract taken 30-60 minutes before bedtime. For anxiety, 200-400 mg may be taken up to three times daily. Continuous use beyond 4-6 weeks should be evaluated by a healthcare provider. Contraindications include hepatic impairment due to rare hepatotoxicity reports with certain preparations. Drug interactions may occur with other CNS depressants, anesthetics, and barbiturates. Valerian has been studied in combination with hops for insomnia, with clinical trials showing the combination may be as effective as benzodiazepines for sleep quality improvement without dependency risk. The herb has been investigated for menstrual cramp relief, with a clinical trial showing reduced pain severity in primary dysmenorrhea. Valerian aqueous extracts have demonstrated smooth muscle relaxant properties in gastrointestinal preparations. The sedative mechanism appears to involve both GABA-A receptor modulation and adenosine receptor activity. Pharmacokinetic studies show valerenic acid reaches peak plasma levels within 1-2 hours of oral administration. Quality control challenges exist due to the volatile nature of active compounds, with significant degradation during storage and processing.

name: St. John's Wort
title: Hypericum perforatum — WHO Monograph
text: St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) is one of the best-studied herbal medicines for depression. The active constituents include hypericin and hyperforin, which modulate multiple neurotransmitter systems. Clinical trials have demonstrated efficacy equivalent to SSRIs for mild to moderate depression, with fewer side effects. Meta-analyses including over 5000 patients confirm superiority over placebo and non-inferiority to standard antidepressants in mild-to-moderate major depressive disorder. The mechanism involves inhibition of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine reuptake, as well as GABA-A and glutamate receptor modulation by hyperforin. St. John's Wort is a potent inducer of cytochrome P450 3A4 and P-glycoprotein, leading to numerous clinically significant drug interactions. Affected medications include oral contraceptives, cyclosporine, warfarin, HIV protease inhibitors, and many others. Photosensitivity may occur, particularly in fair-skinned individuals at high doses. The recommended dosage is 300 mg of standardized extract containing 0.3 percent hypericin three times daily. Onset of antidepressant effect typically requires 4-6 weeks. Contraindications include concurrent use of prescription antidepressants due to risk of serotonin syndrome, bipolar disorder, and planned surgery. The WHO emphasizes the critical importance of disclosure to healthcare providers. Research has also explored the anxiolytic properties of St. John's Wort, with clinical studies showing benefit in generalized anxiety disorder and social anxiety disorder. The anti-inflammatory and wound healing properties have been documented in topical applications, where hyperforin demonstrates antibacterial activity and promotes tissue regeneration. Preliminary studies have investigated the antiviral properties, particularly against enveloped viruses. The herb has shown neuroprotective effects in animal models of traumatic brain injury and stroke. Recent pharmacogenomic research has identified genetic factors that may predict individual response to St. John's Wort therapy. The herb's interaction with the pregnane X receptor has been identified as the primary mechanism for CYP3A4 induction. Despite the drug interaction concerns, St. John's Wort remains one of the most commonly used herbal medicines in Europe, where it is available by prescription in Germany.

name: Chamomile
title: Matricaria chamomilla — WHO Monograph
text: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla, also known as Matricaria recutita) flower heads are one of the most widely consumed herbal teas globally. The key bioactive compounds include the terpenoid bisabolol and the flavonoid apigenin. Apigenin binds to benzodiazepine receptors, producing mild sedative and anxiolytic effects without significant CNS depression. Clinical trials have demonstrated anxiolytic efficacy in generalized anxiety disorder, with significant reductions in Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale scores compared to placebo. Chamomile exhibits anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties in the gastrointestinal tract, making it useful for functional dyspepsia, colic, and irritable bowel syndrome. Topical preparations are used for wound healing, eczema, and mucositis, with evidence supporting anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity. The anti-diabetic potential has been explored in preliminary studies showing modest reductions in HbA1c and fasting glucose. The recommended dosage for tea preparation is 1-4 cups daily using 3 grams of dried flowers per cup. Standardized extract dosing for anxiety is 220-1100 mg daily. Contraindications include known allergy to Asteraceae family plants. Chamomile may potentiate anticoagulant effects and interact with CYP3A4 substrates. The safety profile is excellent, making it suitable for long-term use. Chamomile has been studied for sleep quality improvement, with clinical trials showing reduced sleep latency and improved overall sleep quality in elderly populations. The herb has traditional use as a carminative and digestive aid, with pharmacological studies confirming spasmolytic effects on intestinal smooth muscle mediated through calcium channel modulation. In dermatology, chamomile preparations have demonstrated efficacy comparable to hydrocortisone for mild eczema and dermatitis. The essential oil has shown antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Candida albicans, and other common skin pathogens. Research has explored chamomile as a mouthwash for oral mucositis prevention in cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy, with promising results. Inhalation of chamomile essential oil has been studied for anxiety reduction in clinical settings, with aromatherapy trials showing modest anxiolytic effects. The herb contains coumarin compounds, which contribute to the theoretical anticoagulant interaction risk.

name: Peppermint
title: Mentha x piperita — WHO Monograph
text: Peppermint (Mentha x piperita) leaf and essential oil have been used in traditional medicine for digestive complaints across European and Asian traditions. The primary active constituent is menthol, comprising 30-55 percent of the essential oil. Enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules are the most studied formulation for irritable bowel syndrome. Meta-analyses of clinical trials demonstrate significant improvement in global IBS symptoms, abdominal pain, and bloating compared to placebo. The antispasmodic mechanism involves calcium channel antagonism in intestinal smooth muscle, reducing colonic motility and spasm. Peppermint oil also exhibits antimicrobial activity against a range of gastrointestinal pathogens including Helicobacter pylori. Topical application of diluted peppermint oil provides analgesic effects for tension headaches, with efficacy comparable to acetaminophen in controlled studies. The menthol component activates TRPM8 cold receptors, producing the characteristic cooling sensation and mild local anesthetic effect. Recommended dosage for IBS is 0.2-0.4 mL of enteric-coated oil three times daily between meals. For headache, 10 percent peppermint oil solution is applied topically to temples. Contraindications include gastroesophageal reflux disease, as peppermint relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter. Hiatal hernia and severe liver disease are additional contraindications. Drug interactions are minimal at standard doses. Peppermint oil has been studied for its effects on cognitive performance and alertness, with some studies showing improved memory and sustained attention with peppermint aroma exposure. The oil has demonstrated antifungal activity against dermatophytes and Candida species. In respiratory applications, menthol is widely used as a nasal decongestant, though the mechanism is primarily through stimulation of cold receptors rather than actual reduction of nasal airway resistance. Peppermint oil has been investigated as an antispasmodic during colonoscopy procedures, with studies showing reduced colonic spasm when applied directly to the colonic mucosa. The herb has traditional use for nausea, though clinical evidence for this application is limited compared to ginger. Safety concerns primarily relate to menthol sensitivity and the risk of esophageal reflux with non-enteric-coated preparations.
